description.p1: My future, my desire to choose myself and live selfishly as my most authentic self. My desire to be the best person I can be, to grow and love more than I’ve loved before. I remind myself that I am not working, studying, and living for anyone else but myself and the beings I love. It is ugly, it is poorly put together, but it is mine.

My home, where I was born, raised, loved, and hated. I have complicated feelings about my city. I love it and will defend it with my life, but at the same time, I don’t think it will be my forever home. Even though every year that passes feels like I’m outgrowing it more and more, I will be a New Yorker till the day I die. It would be insane to mention NYC without also mentioning LaGuardia High School, my alma mater. It was there that I gained my dearest friends, but also where I tied my art to my academics in an official capacity. I will never regret my time there, but I always wonder what would’ve been different if I had chosen another school.

An everlasting presence throughout my life has always been my queerness. It is something that I discovered young (thank you to Shakira and Rihanna’s ''Can't Remember to Forget You'') but took a long time to accept. High school was when I finally was open about it and therefore was as loud about it as possible, so I’ve since calmed down. Even with my boyfriend, I still consider queerness an integral part of my identity- I would not be who I am without that arc.

Highlighting one of the other big influences of my life without mentioning Tokyo Ghoul would be insane. I got into manga and anime quite young, either at the tail end of elementary school or at the beginning of middle school. Sui Ishida’s style was so viscerally beautiful to a young me that although I have grown out of it, I will always admire it. This summer I will reread the series in its entirety, and see if I am still as fond of it as I once was.

A symbol of the promise I am making now to both my past and future self. I want to make the struggles worth it and lay the groundwork for a brighter future. Although it may seem redundant with Collage, this is reflective of the act itself rather than the hope and acceptance, a connector for all the portions of my life.

Thanks to my beloved, I’ve flown back and forth between New York and Ohio for the past two years. It was never a state I thought I would like (still not as good as New York, obviously), but I was amazed by it. Experiencing a small town for the first time in my life, to the endlessly flat Americana that I’ve only ever seen in movies, has undoubtedly widened my worldview. The Midwest won’t be my final destination, but I will always think fondly of Culver’s, Dietsch’s, and so much more.

Not too long after I expressed a desire to draw, my mom provided me with the opportunity to begin art classes. I went on a nearly weekly basis for several years, tapering off once I entered sophomore year of high school. Through it, I established fundamentals young, and by middle school, I was drawing the human figure both clothed and nude. As much as it has helped me (learning contrapposto so young is very useful), this was where my ‘schooling’ began being tied to my art.

The earliest influence for my art is undoubtedly Hasbro’s My Little Pony: Friendship is Magic. The entire reason I started drawing at the wee little age of 8 or 9 was because of a classmate who drew beautifully, and I wanted to be just like her. I never remembered her name, but she quite literally set the trajectory for my life. I kept watching and drawing MLP content until nearly high school, making it one of the few interests I kept for years. The show raised me and became the first instance of the horse constant.

A self-portrait I created back during high school when I was trying to prepare a portfolio for college applications. I made it using a combination of Procreate and {INSERT SOFTWARE} during a time when I felt unsure about my future in the arts. This was one of three self-portraits I created for my portfolio, and it is the only one I still feel resonates with me. I unknowingly drew the despair I only became able to name recently, yet I face the future.

My biggest influence (especially for my adult life), the piece of media that I think matured me more than I ever could have imagined: Houseki no Kuni (HnK) or Land of the Lustrous. Haruko Ichikawa’s ability to craft the most heartbreaking story about humanity while infusing it with incredible cultural influences and references is groundbreaking. I lived the ups and downs of the tumultuous publication cycle towards the end of the manga, and it made the messaging more real than any other media I’ve engaged with. To the nonsensical rockisms I still carry with me.

The role of horses in the media that is dear to me became evidently clear by the time I realized how impactful Bojack Horseman would be to me. Somehow, it is the constant of my life, continuing currently in my never-ending rewatches of Bojack and the game, Umamusume: Pretty Derby. Umamusume has yet to establish itself as a life-changing force, so this image is more representative of the horse constant rather than a specific reference like the others. And of course, Happy Year of the Horse!

The amount of papers I have written for school will always be depressing for me. Coming in for illustration and realizing I wanted to change was a very big pivot for me. I am proud of my time in Design History and Practice, but I will certainly not miss the amount of writing and the lack of the creative expression that I’m used to. I hope with my graduation I can write more freely once again.

Next to HnK, Bojack Horseman lives in my mind often. It’s something I have rewatched and re-experienced so many times, each time as profound as the last. In particular, the balloons are a reference to Season 2 Episode 11, “Escape from L.A.,” in which Bojack becomes guilty of one of the most heinous acts in the show. It is so disturbing, the balloon iconography haunting but so reflective of what the series is at the same time. Bojack Horseman is a show that makes you reckon with the worst parts of yourself and asks you, how do you move on? Bojack became the second of the horse constants.

My soul cat, my son, the most precious being I have ever met. I adopted him at the end of high school, I experienced my entire Parson’s career with him at my side, and I hope I get to experience the birth of my career with him as well. He is my muse, one of the reasons I keep on going, and the fluffiest inconvenience. For him, I welcome many more snotty mornings and “no don’t eat that”s and “oh my god that’s disgusting”. <3

menu.p1: Coupling of Parts is a triptych made out of nearly a decade of being a maker and a creative. Growing up, I made art simply out of the joy of making- not to achieve a deeper meaning or represent something interesting. As I developed and integrated my artwork with my academics, I started to lose myself. I slowly stopped creating selfishly, and by the time I entered Parsons, I ceased making art outside of school entirely. There is an incredible gap and inconsistency in my work. Once my decline spiraled out of control, I faked meanings and processes to appease my professors. Within the past year, however, my determination to prioritize myself again has become resolute. This work was born out of a desire to draw and paint again- not for anyone else, not for a grade or a portfolio, but because creating something beautiful is simply fun. It is interactive simply because I desire it so; I want to present the option of clicking into my life. The chaos is clear in the three phases that, from right to left, reflect my past, my present, and my future. It is filled with references to the pieces of media that have created the person I am, my passions, and my desire to take control of my life, although specifics will be expanded if you click on the individual images. This is private, intimate, you may choose to learn about me, or you may simply enjoy the life I have lived through this triptych. What is paramount though, is that this work presents the disparities in my career while serving as a marker to move forward.
menu.p2: To Yuzu, Mom, Jaret, Cindy, Fadilah, Lily, and all the others that made this possible. Thank you to Margot, Mev, Rory, and all my other professors who guided me. Huge shout-out to Neven for helping me make this, and a last thank you for those who are no longer in my life for one reason or another but shaped me in some way.
menu.p3: Painted in Procreate, {DIMENSIONS}. Made with Figma Make, packaged using GPT-5.4 Pro (thank you again, Neven), edited with Visual Studio Code, and uploaded on GitHub.
